embeddings.content:
# Enterprise Agent Workflow Guide

## System Objective

An enterprise agent workflow system should do more than answer questions. It
should interpret the user request, decide whether knowledge retrieval is
enough, and then choose whether to call a tool, execute a structured task, or
ask for clarification. The workflow layer is responsible for turning language
understanding into controlled system behavior.

## Request Routing

The first step in an agent workflow is request routing. A router examines the
user question and decides which path should handle it. Some requests are
knowledge requests that only require retrieval and answer generation. Some are
execution requests that require tools, external APIs, or database access. Other
requests are ambiguous and should be sent to a clarification step before the
system takes action.

## Retrieval Path

If the router decides that the request is a knowledge question, the workflow
enters the retrieval path. The system embeds the query, retrieves the most
relevant chunks, optionally reranks them, and then sends the ranked context to
the language model. This path is appropriate when the user wants explanation,
documentation lookup, policy guidance, or architectural context.

## Tool Execution Path

If the request requires action, the workflow should move into a tool execution
path. The agent may call a ticketing tool, a deployment tool, a search API, or
an internal service. Tool calls should be structured and validated because the
agent is no longer only producing text; it is now affecting external systems.
The workflow should also record tool inputs, outputs, and execution status.

## Clarification Step

Clarification is necessary when the user intent is underspecified or when the
system lacks enough context to act safely. For example, if a user asks the
agent to create a ticket without naming the service, severity, or environment,
the workflow should ask follow-up questions instead of making risky
assumptions. A strong agent should prefer clarification over incorrect action.

## Failure Handling

A production workflow needs failure handling at every stage. Retrieval may
return weak context. A tool may fail because of authentication, timeout, or bad
parameters. A downstream API may be temporarily unavailable. The workflow
should capture these failures, surface a clear status, and decide whether to
retry, fall back, or ask the user for another input.

## Observability

Observability is critical in an agent workflow system. Engineers should log the
route decision, retrieval latency, tool latency, answer latency, provider
selection, fallback behavior, and final action status. Without these traces, it
is difficult to understand whether a bad answer came from weak retrieval, poor
reasoning, or a failed tool call.

## Evaluation

Workflow evaluation should measure more than answer quality. Engineers should
evaluate routing accuracy, tool selection accuracy, task completion rate, and
failure recovery behavior. A useful evaluation dataset should contain both
knowledge questions and execution-oriented requests so that the workflow can be
tested across different decision paths.
